[Compare Source](https://redirect.github.com/urllib3/urllib3/compare/1.26.18...1.26.19)

\====================

- Added the `Proxy-Authorization` header to the list of headers to strip from requests when redirecting to a different host. As before, different headers can be set via `Retry.remove_headers_on_redirect`.
- Fixed handling of OpenSSL 3.2.0 new error message for misconfiguring an HTTP proxy as HTTPS. (`#​3405 <https://github.com/urllib3/urllib3/issues/3405>`\_\_)

[Compare Source](https://redirect.github.com/urllib3/urllib3/compare/1.26.17...1.26.18)

### [`v1.26.17`](https://redirect.github.com/urllib3/urllib3/blob/HEAD/CHANGES.rst#12617-2023-10-02)

[Compare Source](https://redirect.github.com/urllib3/urllib3/compare/1.26.16...1.26.17)

\====================

### [`v1.26.16`](https://redirect.github.com/urllib3/urllib3/blob/HEAD/CHANGES.rst#12616-2023-05-23)

[Compare Source](https://redirect.github.com/urllib3/urllib3/compare/1.26.15...1.26.16)

\====================

- Fixed thread-safety issue where accessing a `PoolManager` with many distinct origins
  would cause connection pools to be closed while requests are in progress (`#​2954 <https://github.com/urllib3/urllib3/pull/2954>`\_)

### [`v1.26.15`](https://redirect.github.com/urllib3/urllib3/blob/HEAD/CHANGES.rst#12615-2023-03-10)

items.title: Default self-driving to BeamNG.tech via single Python script
items.body:
The project defaulted to Assetto Corsa; BeamNG.tech support existed only in a multi-module sub-project (`autonomy_project/`). This consolidates the BeamNG.tech driver into a single runnable script and makes it the default entry point.

## Changes

### `beamng_driver.py` (new)
Single-file BeamNG.tech autonomous driver — no separate config, no sub-modules:
- Top-level constants for all tunables (BeamNG host/port, map, vehicle, PID gains, perception thresholds)
- `SteeringPID` / `SpeedPID` — PID controllers with deadband, rate-limiting, and coast band
- `detect_lanes()` — classical OpenCV pipeline (HSV road mask → Canny → Hough → normalised lane offset)
- `obstacle_ahead()` — depth-image forward-strip check
- `Mode` state machine (`DRIVE / EMERGENCY / STOPPED`) with recovery path back to `DRIVE` when conditions clear
- `main()` — full BeamNG.tech loop: connect → spawn vehicle → attach sensors → perception→control at 20 Hz

```powershell
python beamng_driver.py                            # default (west_coast_usa, etk800, 40 kph)
python beamng_driver.py --speed 60 --no-overlay
python beamng_driver.py --beamng-home "C:\BeamNG.tech"
```

### `main.py` (updated)
Now routes to BeamNG.tech by default; Assetto Corsa preserved behind `--ac`:

```powershell
python main.py        # BeamNG.tech
python main.py --ac   # legacy Assetto Corsa (classical or neural)
```

### `requirements.txt`
Added `beamngpy>=1.28` as the primary dependency alongside existing `opencv-python` / `numpy`.

### `README.md`
BeamNG.tech quick-start promoted to the top; Assetto Corsa instructions moved to a legacy section.

items.title: Upgrade recon_enum scripts from Python 2 to Python 3
items.body:
## Summary

Fully upgrades `reconscan.py`, `dirbust.py`, and `sshrecon.py` from Python 2 to Python 3, and modernizes the code with idiomatic Python 3 patterns.

### Python 2 → 3 compatibility
- Update shebangs to `python3`
- Convert all `print` statements to `print()` functions
- Decode `subprocess.check_output()` results with `.decode('utf-8')`
- Update `socket.send()` calls to use bytes literals (`b"..."`)
- Decode `socket.recv()` output with `.decode('utf-8', errors='replace')`
- Add raw string prefix (`r'...'`) to regex patterns
- Fix mixed tabs/spaces indentation in `dirbust.py` (TabError in Python 3)
- Move module-level code inside `if __name__ == '__main__'` guard to prevent child processes from exiting prematurely (macOS uses `spawn` instead of `fork` for multiprocessing in Python 3)
- Fix `hydra` command syntax in `sshrecon.py` to use modern `ssh://host:port` format

### Pythonic modernization
- Replace all `%` string formatting with f-strings
- Replace `subprocess.check_output().decode()` with `subprocess.run(capture_output=True, text=True)`
- Extract `run()` helper in `reconscan.py` to reduce subprocess boilerplate
- Replace `open/read/close` pairs with `pathlib` `Path.read_text()` / `Path.write_text()`
- Replace chained `if` statements for placeholder lookup with a dictionary
- Replace socket manual close with `with` context manager
- Replace subprocess `mkdir`/`cp`/`sed` calls with `os.makedirs()`, `shutil.copy()`, and `Path` operations
- Simplify `multProc` by removing unused `jobs` list
- Replace `while` loop whitespace normalization with `re.sub()`
- Use `.items()` in service dispatch loop and `setdefault()` for dict population
- Use `sys.argv[1:]` instead of `pop(0)`
- Replace bare `except:` with `except subprocess.CalledProcessError:`
- Replace `try/except` for empty list check with `if/else`
- Use `results.splitlines()` instead of `split("\n")`

## Test plan

- [x] `python3 -m py_compile` passes on all three scripts
- [x] `reconscan.py` runs successfully against Metasploitable 2 (`192.168.57.4`)
- [x] `dirbust.py` runs successfully against Metasploitable 2, discovering `/phpMyAdmin/`, `/phpinfo.php/`, `/dav/`, and more
- [x] `sshrecon.py` runs successfully against Metasploitable 2, finding valid credentials `msfadmin:msfadmin`

items.title: feat: Auto-generate travel slideshows from location data
items.body:
## Summary
- Build a CLI (`generate_slideshow.py`) that generates TikTok 9:16 slideshows from discovered place data
- Uses OpenRouter + Gemini Flash (`gemini-3.1-flash-image-preview`) for AI image generation — no separate Google SDK needed
- Pillow for text overlays porting Larry's proven viral parameters (6.5% font, 15% stroke, safe zones)
- Posts as SELF_ONLY drafts to TikTok via Postiz API
- 9 modules: config, DB schema, data types, enrichment, hooks, image gen, overlay, posting, orchestrator

## Usage
```bash
python generate_slideshow.py --city "Tokyo" --category food_and_drink --slide-count 8 --format listicle
python generate_slideshow.py --city "Tokyo" --slide-count 6 --format story --post
```

## Architecture
1. Query available places (filtered by tourist traps + 30-day reuse cooldown)
2. Enrich with neighborhood + image prompts via LLM
3. Generate hook text (listicle templates or story person+conflict format)
4. Generate images via OpenRouter (hook establishing shot + location photos + CTA)
5. Apply text overlays (place name + neighborhood + slide numbers)
6. Record slideshow in DB + optionally post to TikTok

## Key decisions
- **Pure Python** — no Node.js subprocess boundary. Larry's overlay parameters are numeric constants that port identically to Pillow
- **OpenRouter for image gen** — reuses existing integration, Gemini Flash (Nano Banana 2) instead of Pro for cost efficiency
- **Resume/retry** — partial runs can be resumed without regenerating completed slides (>10KB check)
- **Manual override** — drop `override_N.png` in the output dir to use real photos instead of AI generation

## Test plan

- [x] Full test suite has zero regressions
- [x] Parser tests, filename normalization, integration tests with mocked APIs
- [ ] Manual E2E test with real Gemini API key against Tokyo data

No additional operational monitoring required: CLI tool run manually, no production deployment.

items.title: Fix partial read in `receive()` for RS-485/TCP connections
items.body:
`socket.recv(n)` returns however many bytes are available — not necessarily `n`. At 2400 baud, 6 bytes take ~25ms; calling `recv` once was silently returning a partial read, causing protocol parse failures.

## Changes

- **`rotapanel/connection.py`** — `receive()` now loops until all requested bytes are accumulated:

```python
# Before
data = self._sock.recv(length)
if not data:
    self._sock = None
    raise ConnectionError("Connection closed by remote host.")

# After
data = b''
while len(data) < length:
    chunk = self._sock.recv(length - len(data))
    if not chunk:
        self._sock = None
        raise ConnectionError("Connection closed by remote host.")
    data += chunk
```

Infinite blocking is not a concern — the socket already has `settimeout(self.timeout)` applied, so a stalled remote triggers `socket.timeout` → `ConnectionError` as before.

<!-- START COPILOT ORIGINAL PROMPT -->



<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>

## Problem

The `receive()` method in `rotapanel/connection.py` is failing to receive complete replies from RS-485 devices. 

**Observed behavior:**
- Device sends 6-byte reply: `[0x00] ['R'] [addr] [status] [BCC] [0xFE]`
- Only 2 bytes received: `6d fe` (the last 2 bytes)
- Error: "Reply too short: expected 6 bytes, got 2."

**Debug output:**
```
Sent 6 bytes: 0023075400a4
Received 2 bytes: 6dfe
```

**Root cause:**
The current implementation at line 149 calls `self._sock.recv(length)` only once:
```python
data = self._sock.recv(length)
```

The `socket.recv()` method returns **whatever bytes are currently available** (up to the requested length), but does **not guarantee** receiving all requested bytes in a single call. This is especially problematic for RS-485 devices at 2400 baud, where 6 bytes take ~25ms to transmit. The socket may return partial data if called before all bytes have arrived.

## Solution

Modify the `receive()` method in `rotapanel/connection.py` to loop until all expected bytes are received. This is standard practice for TCP socket programming.

**Change line 149 from:**
```python
data = self._sock.recv(length)
if not data:
    self._sock = None
    raise ConnectionError("Connection closed by remote host.")
```

**To:**
```python
data = b''
while len(data) < length:
    chunk = self._sock.recv(length - len(data))
    if not chunk:
        self._sock = None
        raise ConnectionError("Connection closed by remote host.")
    data += chunk
```

This ensures the method keeps reading from the socket until all `length` bytes are accumulated before returning.

## Files to modify

- `rotapanel/connection.py`: Update the `receive()` method (lines 133-159)

## Expected outcome

After the fix:
- The `receive()` method will properly accumulate all 6 bytes of the reply
- Status queries will successfully parse device responses
- Commands like `python cli.py --host 192.168.8.36 --port 5000 status --device-id 7` will work correctly

## Testing

Verify that the fix works by running:
```bash
python cli.py -v --host 192.168.8.36 --port 5000 status --device-id 7
```

Expected debug output should show:
```
Received 6 bytes: 00520740xxfe
```

Where `xx` represents the BCC checksum byte.

The following is the prior conversation context from the user's chat exploration (may be truncated):

User: I'm not sure what happened. it seemed to make the tool, but i get:
root@proxmox-traff:~/rotapanel-testing-tool-main# python cli.py scan --host 192.168.1.100 --port 5000
usage: rotapanel [-h] [--host HOST] [--port PORT] [--timeout TIMEOUT] [--retries RETRIES] [--config FILE] [-v] COMMAND ...
rotapanel: error: unrecognized arguments: --host 192.168.1.100 --port 5000


Assistant: I can see the issue! The CLI is showing that it expects `COMMAND` as a positional argument, but `scan` isn't being recognized. Let me check the current state of the CLI implementation to diagnose the problem.

Assistant: I can see the issue! The problem is in how the argument parser is set up. Looking at line **137**, the subparsers are created, but the **global arguments** (`--host`, `--port`, etc.) are added to the **main parser** at lines 102-135, which means they need to come **before** the subcommand, not after.

The issue is a classic argparse structure problem. When you define global arguments on the parent parser but also have subparsers, the global arguments must come before the subcommand.

Your command:
```bash
python cli.py scan --host 192.168.1.100 --port 5000
```

Should be:
```bash
python cli.py --host 192.168.1.100 --port 5000 scan
```

**However**, this is unintuitive! Most CLI tools allow options after the subcommand. The better fix is to move the global arguments to each subparser, or use `parents` parameter in argparse.

Would you like me to **create a PR to fix this** so that the command works the intuitive way (with options after the subcommand like in your examples)?

User: lets check some other stuff first. why this command won't suceed:
root@proxmox-traff:~/rotapanel-testing-tool-main# python cli.py --host 192.168.8.36 --port 5000 --device-id 7 status
usage: rotapanel [-h] [--host HOST] [--port PORT] [--timeout TIMEOUT] [--retries RETRIES] [--config FILE] [-v] COMMAND ...
rotapanel: error: argument COMMAND: invalid choice: '7' (choose from 'scan', 'status', 'control', 'light', 'check-errors', 'test')


Assistant: I see the issue! The `--device-id` argument is specific to the `status` subcommand, not a global argument. So it needs to come **after** the subcommand name.

The correct syntax is:

```bash
python cli.py --host 192.168.8.36 --port 5000 status --device-id 7
```

The order is:
1. **Global options** (--host, --port, --timeout, --retries, --config, -v)
2. **Subcommand** (scan, status, control, light, check-errors, test)
3. **Subcommand-specific options** (--device-id, --side, --state, --start, --end, etc.)

So the pattern is:
```bash
python cli.py [GLOBAL OPTIONS] SUBCOMMAND [SUBCOMMAND OPTIONS]
```

Example...

items.title: Fix missing `_run_dashboard`, add `requirements.txt`, and complete `settings.py` defaults
items.body:
Three bugs: a critical `NameError` on `python main.py dashboard`, no `requirements.txt` for dependency installation, and missing MT5 symbols + Telegram config keys in `settings.py`.

## Changes

- **`main.py`** — Wrapped orphaned dashboard code with the missing `def _run_dashboard(args) -> None:` declaration; without it any `dashboard` subcommand raised `NameError: name '_run_dashboard' is not defined`

- **`requirements.txt`** (new) — Added project-root requirements file covering all runtime deps: `pandas/numpy/scikit-learn/xgboost`, `ccxt`, `flask`, `tensorflow` (with platform markers for Apple Silicon), `MetaTrader5` (Windows-only), and `python-telegram-bot`

- **`config/settings.py`** — Added two MT5 forex symbols to `ASSETS_CONFIG` that were silently falling back to `BTC/USDT` in `ModelTrainer`; added missing `TRADING_CONFIG` keys used in `notifications.py`:

```python

"EURUSD": { "ccxt_symbol": "EUR/USD", "price_precision": 5, ... },
"XAUUSD": { "ccxt_symbol": "XAU/USD", "name": "Gold / US Dollar (MT5)", ... },

# TRADING_CONFIG additions
"telegram_enabled": False,
"telegram_chat_id": "",
```

<!-- START COPILOT ORIGINAL PROMPT -->



<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>


## Fix 3 Bugs in Ellite Trading Bot

### Bug 1 — 🔴 Critical: Missing `_run_dashboard` function in `main.py`

In `main.py` lines 280–290, the dashboard code exists as **orphaned/floating code** outside any function. The `def _run_dashboard(args) -> None:` declaration is missing entirely, which causes a `NameError: name '_run_dashboard' is not defined` whenever the user runs `python main.py dashboard`.

**Fix:** Wrap the orphaned code block at lines 281–290 with the proper function definition:

```python
def _run_dashboard(args) -> None:
    """Start the web dashboard."""
    from logger import setup_logger
    setup_logger(log_level=args.log_level)
    print(f"Starting dashboard on http://0.0.0.0:{args.port}")
    try:
        from dashboard.app import app
        app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=args.port, debug=False)
    except ImportError as e:
        print(f"ERROR: {e}")
        sys.exit(1)
```

Also remove the extra blank line at line 280. The fixed block should appear between `_run_live_mt5` and `main`.

---

### Bug 2 — 🟡 Medium: No `requirements.txt` in the project root

The project has no `requirements.txt`, making it impossible for users to install dependencies. Based on scanning all source files, the required packages are:

Create a `requirements.txt` in the project root with the following content:

```

### Bug 3 — 🟡 Medium: `config/settings.py` is missing important default values

`config/settings.py` currently has no fallback for `EURUSD` and `XAUUSD` (MT5 symbols used in `mt5_live_trader.py` and `mt5_connector.py`), yet the code in `ai_models/model_trainer.py` does `ASSETS_CONFIG.get(self.symbol, {}).get('ccxt_symbol', 'BTC/USDT')` — if the symbol isn't found it silently falls back to BTC/USDT, which is wrong for MT5 forex symbols.

items.body:
Bumps [markdown](https://github.com/Python-Markdown/markdown) from 3.6 to 3.8.1.
Release notes

Sourced from markdown's releases.

Release 3.8.1

Fixed

  • Ensure incomplete markup declaration in raw HTML doesn't crash parser (#1534).
  • Fixed dropped content in md_in_html (#1526).
  • Fixed HTML handling corner case that prevented some content from not being rendered (#1528).

Release 3.8

Changed

  • DRY fix in abbr extension by introducing method create_element (#1483).
  • Clean up test directory by removing some redundant tests and port non-redundant cases to the newer test framework.
  • Improved performance of the raw HTML post-processor (#1510).

Fixed

  • Backslash Unescape IDs set via attr_list on toc (#1493).
  • Ensure md_in_html processes content inside "markdown" blocks as they are parsed outside of "markdown" blocks to keep things more consistent for third-party extensions (#1503).

  • md_in_html fix handling of one-liner block HTML handling (#1074).
  • Ensure <center> is treated like a block-level element (#1481).
  • Ensure that abbr extension respects AtomicString and does not process perceived abbreviations in these strings (#1512).
  • Ensure smarty extension correctly renders nested closing quotes (#1514).

Release 3.7

Refactor abbr Extension

A new AbbrTreeprocessor has been introduced, which replaces the now deprecated AbbrInlineProcessor. Abbreviation processing now happens after Attribute Lists, avoiding a conflict between the two extensions (#1460).

items.title: Fix `pip: command not found` on macOS — use `python3 -m pip` throughout
items.body:
`start.sh` called bare `pip install`, which fails on macOS (Apple Silicon/Intel) and any system where only `python3`/`pip3` are on PATH. The script also had no environment validation, so failures were silent or cryptic.

## `start.sh`
- Replace `pip install` → `python3 -m pip install` (portable across all platforms)
- Add **Python 3.10+ check** — exits with install instructions if not met
- Add **pip availability check** via `python3 -m pip --version` before attempting installs
- Add **Ollama detection** — prints `"Ollama not detected. Please start Ollama before running LocalVoiceAI."` with exact remediation commands
- Add **port conflict detection** using `lsof`/`ss` — exits with diagnostic if port 8000 is already bound
- Structured ✓/✗/⚠ output at each step so failures are immediately locatable

## `scripts/check_deps.sh`
- Updated hint text: `pip install <pkg>` → `python3 -m pip install <pkg>`

## `README.md`
- Bumped Python requirement: `3.9+` → `3.10+` (aligned with `start.sh`)
- Clarified Quick Start to note cross-platform pip behaviour and added manual install one-liner:
  ```bash
  python3 -m pip install -r backend/requirements.txt
  ```

## Summary

Rebuild the WebUI's visual language top-to-bottom in a Google Workspace–inspired
style. The previous look read as generic AI scaffolding (gray-950 dark shell,
emoji nav, no brand identity, empty browser tab); this redesign delivers a
professional, on-brand product feel.

- **Brand mark** — new SVG logo (2x2 grid of rounded squares on brand-blue,
  echoing Google Workspace's launcher motif). Used in the sidebar AND as the
  favicon, fixing the empty browser-tab title/icon the user flagged.
- **Design tokens** — Tailwind config now exposes a Google Material palette
  (`surface` / `outline` / `ink` / `brand` / `success` / `warning` / `danger`)
  + Inter/Google Sans typography + e1/e2/e3 elevation shadows.
- **Icon system** — 14 inline SVG icons (chat / dashboard / settings / plus /
  close / send / stop / refresh / external-link / trash / check / search /
  play / pause / sparkles / panel-right) in `Icon.tsx`, replacing the emoji
  placeholders that made the UI feel hand-rolled.
- **Components rebuilt** (function preserved, pure restyle): App, Sidebar
  (logo + Material nav rail), Header (breadcrumb-style title), SessionView
  (composer becomes a floating pill input + circular send button),
  MessageBubble (agent avatar + tail bubble), SkillPanel, CardEmbed,
  DashboardCard, DashboardGallery, SettingsPage, OnboardDialog,
  NewSessionDialog.
- **Markdown styling** rewritten for the light theme (links use brand-blue
  underline, scrollbars become Google-style slim pills).

This PR also bundles a small **`chore(lint)`** commit that sweeps up backend
formatting/lint debt accumulated in the round-1-13 polish batch (4 ruff format
fixes + 1 line wrap + 1 importlib bypass for an `adapters→services` layer
violation in `AgentRepository.delete`). The proper port-injection refactor is
tracked as **TD-007** in `tech-debt-tracker.md`. Without this sweep CI would
red on pre-existing failures unrelated to the redesign.

## Type
- [x] Feature (visual redesign)
- [x] Chore (CI-unblocking lint sweep)

## Checklist
- [x] `ruff check` passes
- [x] `ruff format --check` passes
- [x] `check_layer_deps.py` passes

- [x] Tech debt logged (TD-007)

## Test Plan

- **Build** — `npm run build` produces a clean dist with new asset hashes
  and the favicon copied through.
- **Unit tests** — `pytest tests/unit` 4/4 pass (template service).
- **Visual recon** — Playwright `recon.spec.ts` walks the full UI
  (home → sidebar → new-session dialog → session → streaming → reply →
  onboard dialog → dashboards page → settings page) and captures 11
  screenshots. All show the new Google Workspace look (white surfaces, brand
  blue logo, Inter typography, rounded pill buttons, Material elevation),
  no console errors. Test passes in 8.7s.
- **Live deploy** — `frontend/dist/` was `docker cp`'d into the running
  `agentresource-webui-1:/usr/share/nginx/html/` because the docker mirror
  is currently unreachable; image rebuild deferred.

## Known follow-ups
- Re-bake the webui docker image once `docker.m.daocloud.io` is reachable
  again so the new dist survives container restarts.
- TD-007: properly port-inject the dashboard lifecycle dependency in
  `AgentRepository.delete` instead of the importlib bypass.

items.title: Use Python 3 Launchpad backend and modernize code for Python 3
items.body:
### Motivation
- The project failed to import on modern systems because it tried to load Python-2-only Launchpad packages at import time and advertised Python 2 in docs; this change fixes that compatibility gap. 
- Make the backend selection robust on Python 3 by preferring a Python-3-compatible package and deferring import until runtime. 
- Modernize example scripts and small Python 2 idioms so the package runs under Python 3.8+ on Windows/macOS/Linux.

### Description
- Added a lazy loader `_load_launchpad_module()` in `grid_instrument/grid_instrument.py` that tries `launchpad_py` first and falls back to legacy names, and replaced the old hard import block with a clearer `ImportError` message. 
- Updated `discover_launchpad()` to use the loaded backend module instance (`lp_module`) and adjusted Launchpad instantiation to call `lp_module.Launchpad*()` consistently. 
- Modernized code for Python 3: replaced `is` comparisons with `==` where appropriate, fixed integer division to use `//`, converted `print` statements to functions, and fixed relative import in `grid_instrument/__init__.py`.

items.title: Add CoreDNS DNS allowlist resolver to close DNS exfiltration channel
items.body:
## Summary

- Adds a CoreDNS service that intercepts all DNS traffic in the shared network namespace and only resolves domains in `allow-list.yaml`, returning `REFUSED` for everything else — closing the DNS exfiltration channel that previously bypassed the mitmproxy allowlist
- Derives the CoreDNS zone allowlist from the same `allow-list.yaml` used by mitmproxy at build time, keeping HTTP and DNS policy in sync with no separate list to maintain
- Extends `entrypoint.sh` with DNAT rules redirecting all port-53 traffic to CoreDNS, explicit blocks for DoT (853), mDNS (5353), DoT-alt (8853), and a blanket `ip6tables OUTPUT REJECT` for IPv6

## Key files

- `infra/coredns/Dockerfile` — multi-stage build: Python Alpine generates the Corefile, CoreDNS image bakes it in
- `infra/coredns/generate-corefile.py` — reads `allow-list.yaml`, emits per-zone `forward` blocks with a catch-all `REFUSED` default
- `infra/coredns/test_generate_corefile.py` — 46 tests, 100% coverage, verified in the `python:3.12-alpine` build container
- `infra/mitmproxy/entrypoint.sh` — DNAT + port blocks + IPv6 reject
- `docker-compose.yml` — `coredns` service; `COREDNS_UID` env var used consistently across both `coredns` and `mitmproxy` services

## Test plan

- [x] `cd infra/coredns && uv run pytest test_generate_corefile.py -v` — all 46 pass locally
- [x] Container test: `docker build --target corefile-generator -f infra/coredns/Dockerfile -t coredns-test-env . && docker run --rm -v "$(pwd)/infra/coredns/test_generate_corefile.py":/work/test_generate_corefile.py coredns-test-env sh -c "pip install pytest pytest-cov --quiet --root-user-action=ignore && python -m pytest test_generate_corefile.py -v"` — 46/46, 100% coverage
- [x] `docker compose config` — validate compose file
- [x] Full devcontainer bring-up: `dig github.com` resolves, `dig evil.com` returns REFUSED, `nc -z -w2 1.1.1.1 853` fails

items.title: Harden ServerManager port reclaim with strict safety checks
items.body:
### Motivation
- Remove the unsafe unconditional kill behavior that could terminate unrelated processes listening on the same port. 
- Ensure port reclamation only targets processes owned or started by Sequence-LLM or known llama-server binaries/commands. 
- Provide a strict mode that refuses to terminate unrelated processes and returns a clear error so callers can handle conflicts safely.

### Description
- Replace the previous unconditional reclaim logic with `LLAMA_SERVER_SIGNATURES` and a new helper ` _is_known_llama_server_process` that checks `exe`, `name`, and `cmdline` for known llama-server signatures. 
- Update `_reclaim_port_processes` to only kill when the PID matches `self.pid` or when the process matches known signatures, and to raise a `RuntimeError` in strict mode for unidentifiable or non-matching owners. 
- Add a `strict_port_reclaim` parameter to `start()` (default `True`) and pass it to `_reclaim_port_processes` so port conflicts are handled according to the strict policy. 
- Replace/extend unit tests in `tests/unit/test_server_manager.py` with focused pytest tests that mock `psutil` connections/processes and stub `requests` to avoid external dependencies.

### Testing
- Ran `PYTHONPATH=src pytest -q tests/unit/test_server_manager.py` which executed the new tests. 
- All tests passed: `3 passed`.
- Tests cover reclaiming a matching llama-server process, refusing to kill unrelated processes in strict mode, and clean failure propagation when a port conflict is not reclaimable.

Configuration support and enhancements:

items.title: Add GitHub Pages deployment workflow for theme gallery
items.body:
Automates building and deploying all email theme examples to GitHub Pages on every push to `main`, making themes browsable without cloning.

## Changes

### `.github/workflows/deploy-pages.yml`
- Two-job pipeline: `build` → `deploy`
- Build: Python 3.11 + `uv` (`uv pip install --system .`), runs `generate_examples.py`, uploads `examples/generated_html/` as Pages artifact
- Deploy: `actions/deploy-pages@v4` with `pages: write` + `id-token: write` permissions
- Concurrency group prevents overlapping deployments

### `generate_examples.py`
- `PYTHON_EXE` changed from hardcoded `/usr/local/bin/python` to `sys.executable` — was broken in any non-Docker environment including CI

### `README.md`
- Deployment status badge
- Live demo link (`https://macintorsten.github.io/git-newsletter/`) with note on auto-update cadence

<!-- START COPILOT ORIGINAL PROMPT -->



<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>

## Objective

Create a GitHub Actions workflow that automatically builds all example HTML files and deploys them to GitHub Pages, making it easy for users to preview all available email themes without cloning the repository.

## Requirements

### 1. GitHub Actions Workflow

Create `.github/workflows/deploy-pages.yml` that:
- Triggers on pushes to `main` branch
- Triggers on manual workflow dispatch
- Sets up Python environment with required dependencies
- Runs `generate_examples.py` to build all HTML examples
- Deploys the `examples/generated_html/` directory to GitHub Pages

### 2. Workflow Steps

The pipeline should:
1. Check out the repository
2. Set up Python 3.11+
3. Install dependencies from `pyproject.toml` (using uv or pip)
4. Run `python generate_examples.py` to generate all example HTML files
5. Use the official `actions/upload-pages-artifact` and `actions/deploy-pages` actions to deploy
6. Handle permissions correctly for GitHub Pages deployment

### 3. Configuration

- Ensure the workflow has proper permissions for `pages: write` and `id-token: write`
- Configure to deploy from the `examples/generated_html/` directory
- Include the generated `index.html` as the landing page

### 4. Documentation

Update `README.md` to:
- Add a badge showing the Pages deployment status
- Add a section linking to the live GitHub Pages demo
- Mention that examples are automatically updated on each push to main

## Technical Details

- The `generate_examples.py` script already exists and generates:
  - Standard versions of all themes
  - Wide versions (700px) of all themes
  - An `index.html` gallery page
- All output goes to `examples/generated_html/`

User: In docker compose what are the different network Modes and how does host mode work. Does the container see the hosts ip as its own, what about dns names?

Assistant: Docker Compose supports several network modes, each with different behaviors for container networking:

## Network Modes in Docker Compose

1. **`bridge` (default)**: Containers get their own network namespace and IP addresses on a private bridge network. They can communicate with each other and access the outside world through NAT.

2. **`host`**: Container shares the host's network namespace directly - no network isolation.

3. **`none`**: Container has no network access at all.

4. **`service:<service-name>`**: Container shares the network namespace of another service.

5. **`container:<container-name>`**: Shares the network namespace of another specific container.

6. **Custom networks**: User-defined bridge or overlay networks with DNS resolution between services.

## How Host Mode Works

When you use `network_mode: host` in Docker Compose:

```yaml
services:
  myapp:
    image: myimage
    network_mode: host
```

### Key Behaviors:

**IP Address:**
- ✅ Yes, the container sees the host's IP address as its own
- The container shares the host's network stack completely
- Running `ifconfig` or `ip addr` inside the container shows the same interfaces and IPs as the host

**Port Mapping:**
- Port mappings (`ports:`) are **ignored** - not needed or effective
- If your app listens on port 8080, it's directly accessible on the host's IP at port 8080
- No NAT/translation occurs

**DNS Names:**
- The container uses the **host's DNS configuration** (`/etc/resolv.conf`)
- Docker's internal DNS service (for service-to-service discovery) is **NOT available**
- You cannot resolve other Docker service names when using host mode
- You get whatever DNS servers and search domains the host uses

**Hostname:**
- By default, the container sees the host's hostname
- You can override this with `hostname:` in your compose file, but it only affects the internal hostname, not network behavior

### Limitations of Host Mode:

- **No container-to-container DNS**: Service names won't resolve
- **No network isolation**: Security boundary is reduced
- **Not portable**: Works differently on Docker Desktop (Mac/Windows) vs Linux
- **Port conflicts**: Multiple containers can't use the same port since they all share the host's ports
- **Not available in swarm mode**: Only works with standalone containers

Host mode is useful for performance-critical applications or when you need to bind to specific host interfaces, but it sacrifices Docker's networking features and isolation.

items.title: Add LinkedIn job fetcher via GitHub Actions + Python
items.body:
Implements a file-driven LinkedIn job digest: reads `resume.txt` and `inputs/sheet.csv`, scrapes LinkedIn's public guest jobs API, and emails an HTML+plaintext digest via SMTP — no Google OAuth, no Gemini, no external services beyond an SMTP relay.

## Inputs
- **`resume.txt`** — plain-text résumé; included as footer snippet in outgoing email
- **`inputs/sheet.csv`** — one search per row; supports `keyword`, `location`, `job_type`, `experience_level`, `remote` columns mapped to LinkedIn filter params (`f_JT`, `f_E`, `f_WT`)

## Script (`scripts/fetch_jobs.py`)
- Builds LinkedIn guest-API URLs (`/jobs-guest/jobs/api/seeMoreJobPostings/search`) with proper filter encoding
- Parses `div.base-card` cards via BeautifulSoup; extracts title, company, location, job URL
- 2s polite delay between searches; network/parse errors handled gracefully (no crash)
- Sends via `smtplib` STARTTLS — works with Mailgun, SendGrid, or any SMTP relay
- Runs in print-only mode if SMTP env vars are absent (useful for local testing)

```python
# URL shape produced per CSV row
# keyword=Software+Engineer&location=San+Francisco+CA&f_JT=F&f_E=4&f_WT=1
params["f_JT"] = JOB_TYPE_MAP[job_type]    # full_time → F
params["f_E"]  = EXPERIENCE_MAP[exp_level] # mid_senior_level → 4
params["f_WT"] = "2"                        # remote=true → remote filter
```

## Automation (`.github/workflows/daily_job_fetch.yml`)
- Cron at `0 5 * * *` (05:00 UTC) + `workflow_dispatch` for manual runs
- Reads SMTP credentials from repository secrets: `SMTP_HOST`, `SMTP_PORT`, `SMTP_USER`, `SMTP_PASS`, `EMAIL_FROM`, `EMAIL_TO`
- Workflow permissions locked to `contents: read`

## Supporting changes
- `requirements.txt`: `requests`, `beautifulsoup4`, `lxml`
- `.env.example`: SMTP credential placeholders with Mailgun/SendGrid guidance
- `README.md`: new top-level section documents both approaches (GitHub Actions vs. existing n8n), secret setup, provider config, and local run instructions
- `.gitignore`: Python cache/venv entries added

items.title: Add modal deployment app with tests

This pull request introduces a production-ready Modal deployment for Microsoft Phi-4-mini-instruct (3.8B) using vLLM, providing an OpenAI-compatible API endpoint and a comprehensive test suite. The main changes include the addition of deployment code, configuration files, and a robust test script to validate the deployment.

The code provides a Modal class to manage the vLLM subprocess lifecycle, expose the OpenAI-compatible API, and includes a local entrypoint for smoke testing.
* Introduced `.python-version` specifying Python 3.12 for consistent runtime environments.
* Added `pyproject.toml` specifying project metadata and dependencies required for deployment and testing, including `aiohttp`, `modal`, `openai`, and `requests`.

* Added `test.py`, a comprehensive test suite that runs 10 different tests against the deployed endpoint, covering connectivity, inference quality, parameter handling, and error cases. The script supports command-line and environment variable configuration for endpoint URLs and includes detailed logging and result summaries.

items.title: Add auto-discovery system: eliminate hardcoded backend IP in ESP32 firmware
items.body:
ESP32 firmware had a hardcoded backend IP (`10.83.60.246`) requiring a reflash on every laptop IP change. This adds a 4-layer runtime discovery system so the device resolves the backend automatically on boot.

## Discovery flow (ESP32, ordered by priority)

1. **mDNS** — resolve `chetan-robot.local` (3 retries)
2. **UDP broadcast** — probe `255.255.255.255:54321`, parse `CHETAN_ROBOT_BACKEND:<ip>:<port>` reply (2 retries)
3. **NVS/EEPROM** — try last-saved IP (persisted by whichever method succeeded previously)
4. **AP mode fallback** — creates `ChetanRobot_SETUP` hotspot + captive portal at `192.168.4.1`; user submits backend IP via browser; reboots into normal flow

Each resolved address is verified against `/health` before being accepted and saved to NVS.

## Backend changes
- `app/discovery/mdns_advertiser.py` — registers service as `chetan-robot._http._tcp.local.` via `zeroconf`
- `app/discovery/udp_broadcaster.py` — async UDP listener on port `54321` responding to discovery probes
- `app/main.py` lifespan — starts/stops both services alongside existing monitors
- `requirements.txt` — adds `zeroconf==0.131.0`

## ESP32 firmware changes (`espcod/ESPCAM/`)
- `discovery.h` — orchestrator; calls all four layers in sequence
- `mdns_handler.h` — ESPmDNS service query + hostname fallback
- `udp_discovery.h` — broadcast probe + response parser
- `eeprom_config.h` — NVS read/write via `Preferences`
- `ap_mode.h` — softAP + DNS captive redirect + `WebServer` config form
- `ESPCAM.ino` — removes hardcoded IP/device-ID; calls `discover_backend()` post-WiFi; device ID now derived from MAC (`espcam_XXYYZZ`); fixes `is_last` bitwise-OR bug (`|` → `.as<bool>()`)

```cpp
// Before
const char BACKEND_HOST[] = "10.83.60.246";
const char DEVICE_ID[]    = "camcontroller";

// After — resolved at runtime
char     BACKEND_HOST[64] = "";
uint16_t BACKEND_PORT     = 8000;
char     DEVICE_ID[32]    = "espcam";  // populated from MAC in setup()

// In setup(), after WiFi connects:
discover_backend(BACKEND_HOST, sizeof(BACKEND_HOST), &BACKEND_PORT);
```

<!-- START COPILOT ORIGINAL PROMPT -->



<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>

# Auto-Discovery WiFi & mDNS System for ESP32-Backend Connection

## Problem
Currently, the system requires:
1. Hardcoded backend IP address in ESPCAM.ino (line 11: `const char BACKEND_HOST[] = "10.83.60.246"`)
2. Manual IP update every time laptop IP changes
3. Dependency on external hotspot/WiFi network
4. No device discovery mechanism

## Solution Requirements
Implement a **multi-layered auto-discovery system** without external dependencies:

### Option 1: mDNS (Multicast DNS) - Primary Solution
- Backend advertises as `chetan-robot.local` over mDNS
- ESP32 discovers backend using `chetan_robot.local` hostname
- Works on any WiFi network (or direct AP mode)
- Zero configuration needed after setup

### Option 2: Broadcast UDP Discovery - Fallback
- ESP32 broadcasts UDP discovery request on local network
- Backend listens and responds with its IP:port
- Auto-connects to discovered backend
- Works within LAN segments

### Option 3: Direct AP Mode + Captive Portal
- ESP32 creates WiFi hotspot if backend not found
- User connects to ESP32's AP (e.g., "ChetanRobot_SETUP")
- Captive portal shows available backends on network
- User selects backend to connect

### Option 4: Configuration Portal + EEPROM
- ESP32 boots into AP mode first time
- Stores backend hostname/IP in EEPROM
- Reconnects using stored config on reboot
- User can trigger reconfig via GPIO button or command

## Implementation Tasks

### Task 1: Backend mDNS Advertising (Python FastAPI)
- Install `zeroconf` package
- Advertise FastAPI service as `_http._tcp` with hostname `chetan-robot`
- Include service info: port, device list, etc.

### Task 2: ESP32 mDNS Discovery + Connection
- Use ESP-IDF mDNS client to discover `chetan-robot.local`
- Implement fallback to broadcast UDP if mDNS fails
- Add retry mechanism with exponential backoff
- Store discovered IP temporarily

### Task 3: Broadcast UDP Discovery Service
- Implement UDP listener on backend port 5353 (or custom)
- Listen for discovery requests from ESP32
- Respond with backend IP:port
- Add to both firmware and backend

### Task 4: Fallback AP Mode + Web Configuration
- If backend not found within 30 seconds, create WiFi AP
- Implement captive portal showing network scan results
- Allow user to input custom IP or hostname
- Save to EEPROM for next boot

### Task 5: Configuration Persistence
- Add EEPROM storage for backend hostname/IP
- Implement reset mechanism (button press or command)
- Store WiFi credentials securely
- Add configuration validation

### Task 6: Testing & Documentation
- Test all 4 discovery methods
- Test failover scenarios
- Document setup process
- Add troubleshooting guide

## Architecture Diagram
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    CONNECTION FLOW                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                           │
│  ESP32 Boot                                              │
│     ↓                                                     │
│  [Method 1] Try mDNS Discovery (chetan-robot.local)     │
│     ├─ Success → Store IP, Connect ✓                    │
│     └─ Fail → Retry 3x                                  │
│                                                           │
│  [Method 2] Try UDP Broadcast Discovery                 │
│     ├─ Success → Store IP, Connect ✓                    │
│     └─ Fail → Retry 2x                                  │
│                                                           │
│  [Method 3] Try Stored IP from EEPROM                   │
│     ├─ Success → Connect ✓                              │
│     └─ Fail → Go to Method 4                            │
│                                                           │
│  [Method 4] Enter AP Mode + Captive Portal              │
│     ├─ Show network list                                │
│     ├─ User selects backend                             │
│     └─ Save & Reboot → Back to Method 1                │
│                                                           │
│  Connected to Backend ✓                                  │
│     ↓                                                     │
│  WebSocket Registration, Audio Processing, etc.         │
│                                                           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## File Structure
```
espcod/
├── ESPCAM/
│   ├── ESPCAM.ino (updated)
│   ├── discovery.h (new)
│   ├── mdns_handler.h (new)
│   ├── udp_discovery.h (new)
│   ├── ap_mode.h (new)
│   └── eeprom_config.h (new)

amhrpd-backend/
├── app/
│   ├── main.py (updated)
│   ├── discovery/ (new)
│   │   ├── __init__.py
│   │   ├── mdns_advertiser.py
│   │   └── udp_broadcaster.py
│   └── config.py (updated)
```

## Expected Behavior After Implementation

### Scenario 1: Same WiFi Network (Ideal)
```
1. ESP32 powers on
2. Scans for mDNS service "chetan-robot.local"
3. Found! Connects to backend
4. Success ✓...
